display.purpose: Applied to the entire <body> in app/layout.tsx, so it carries headlines AND dense UI, labels, and data alike: one voice doing two incompatible jobs.

body.purpose: Inherited Tailwind sans stack; carries nearly all UI text and prose.

label.purpose: Token counts, IDs, code-like values; the one place a distinct voice appears.

shadows.name: glass-panel
shadows.purpose: Default elevated-surface treatment, paired with backdrop-filter: blur(12px) and rgba(25,33,46,0.7) fill. Reflexive, not purposeful.

shadows.name: shadow-2xl-overlay
shadows.purpose: Tailwind default shadow, sometimes stacked on top of glass surfaces producing double elevation.

motion.name: ease-default
motion.purpose: Generic CSS ease used across glass-button and resize-handle transitions; no custom easing curve defined anywhere.

motion.name: duration-fast
motion.purpose: Hover and focus state transitions on glass surfaces and grid items.

components.name: Glass Panel (default card)
components.description: The default elevated-surface treatment applied to nearly any raised element regardless of context.
components.css: .ds-glass-panel { background: rgba(25, 33, 46, 0.7); backdrop-filter: blur(12px); -webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(12px); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08); border-radius: 16px; padding: 16px; color: #f8fafc; } .ds-glass-panel__title { font-weight: 600; font-size: 0.95rem; margin: 0 0 4px; color: #f8fafc; } .ds-glass-panel__body { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #94a3b8; margin: 0; }

components.name: Primary Button (indigo)
components.description: The most common interactive accent button; competes with violet and purple variants elsewhere for the same role.
components.css: .ds-btn-primary { background: #6366f1; color: #f8fafc; padding: 10px 20px; border-radius: 8px; border: none; font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 500; transition: background 0.2s ease; } .ds-btn-primary:hover { background: #4f46e5; } .ds-btn-primary:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6366f1; outline-offset: 2px; }

components.name: Glass Button (ghost)
components.description: The default secondary/ghost interactive surface; a lighter-weight repeat of the glass-panel treatment.
components.css: .ds-btn-ghost { background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05); backdrop-filter: blur(4px); -webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(4px); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05); color: #f8fafc; padding: 10px 20px; border-radius: 8px; font-size: 0.875rem; transition: all 0.2s ease; } .ds-btn-ghost:hover { background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); border-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); }

components.name: Text Input
components.description: Dark fill input with thin border; focus ring present in roughly a quarter of usages, absent elsewhere.
components.html: <input class="ds-input" type="text" placeholder="Describe what you want to visualize..." />
components.css: .ds-input { background: #1e293b; border: 1px solid #3f3f46; border-radius: 8px; color: #f8fafc; padding: 10px 14px; font-size: 0.875rem; width: 100%; transition: border-color 0.2s ease, box-shadow 0.2s ease; } .ds-input::placeholder { color: #71717a; } .ds-input:focus-visible { outline: none; border-color: #6366f1; box-shadow: 0 0 0 3px rgba(99, 102, 241, 0.25); }

components.name: Sidebar Nav Item
components.description: Navigation chrome rendered with the same glass treatment as content cards, so it doesn't read as structurally distinct.
components.html: Dashboard
components.css: .ds-nav-item { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 10px; padding: 10px 14px; border-radius: 8px; color: #94a3b8; font-size: 0.875rem; text-decoration: none; transition: background 0.2s ease, color 0.2s ease; } .ds-nav-item__icon { width: 18px; height: 18px; flex-shrink: 0; } .ds-nav-item:hover { background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05); color: #f8fafc; } .ds-nav-item--active { background: rgba(99, 102, 241, 0.12); color: #f8fafc; }

components.name: Mono Label Chip
components.description: The one place a distinct typographic voice (monospace) shows up, used for token counts and IDs.
components.css: .ds-chip { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.02em; color: #94a3b8; background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08); border-radius: 9999px; padding: 4px 10px; display: inline-block; }

narrative.overview: This document captures the system as it exists today, a snapshot of "before," not a spec to extend. It is the out-of-the-box dark SaaS template look applied without a unifying point of view: a slate-900 base, an indigo accent borrowed from the Tailwind palette, glass-panel blur on every elevated surface, and Space Grotesk dropped onto headings because it reads as "modern." None of these were wrong choices in isolation. The problem is that no one decision was made in relation to the others, so the system never cohered into something that looks like VisualMe rather than any other AI-tool starter. The clearest symptom is the gray scale: slate and zinc are both in active use across the same screens, sometimes on adjacent elements, alongside three different purples (indigo-500, violet-500, purple-500) that aren't differentiated by role. Buttons, cards, and panels are templated and interchangeable: the same glass-card-with-blur treatment appears regardless of what it contains or what the user is trying to do there, so nothing on screen is purpose-built.
keyCharacteristics: Two competing neutral scales (slate and zinc) used interchangeably with no rule for which applies where
Three accent purples (indigo-500, violet-500, purple-500) doing the same job in different places
Glassmorphism (backdrop-filter: blur) as the default elevation strategy for nearly every surface
A display typeface (Space Grotesk) applied to the entire <body>, carrying dense UI and data, not just the headline moments it suits
No shared button/card/input components; each screen reimplements its own version of the same primitives

rules.name: The Two-Scale Problem
rules.body: slate and zinc are both load-bearing neutral families in this codebase. Wherever you touch a surface, check which scale its siblings use before adding a third option; do not introduce a fourth neutral family.

rules.name: The One-Voice-Two-Jobs Rule
rules.body: A typeface chosen for its personality in headlines will fight the product register's core requirement, the tool should disappear into the task, when it is also asked to carry labels, data, and forms. Display character and informational neutrality are different jobs; they need different faces, or at minimum different weights and tracking, applied deliberately rather than by inheritance.

rules.name: The Reflex Blur Rule
rules.body: Glassmorphism is currently the answer to "how should this surface stand out," applied without asking whether the surface needs to feel layered over moving content (its only legitimate use) or simply distinct from its neighbor (which a flat fill and a 1px border accomplish without the performance cost of backdrop-filter).

dos: Do pick one neutral family (slate or zinc, not both) before touching any surface, and migrate its neighbors to match rather than adding a third option.
Do collapse the three competing purples (indigo-500, violet-500, purple-500) into a single deliberate accent with named roles, per PRODUCT.md's call for visual confidence and a real point of view.

Do treat .glass-panel / .glass-button as defaults to question, not defaults to extend; reach for a flat fill plus a hairline border first and reserve blur for surfaces that genuinely float over moving content.
Do give display and body type separate jobs: a confident display voice for headline moments, a quieter denser-friendly face for labels, forms, and data, so the tool can disappear into the task.
donts: Don't add a sixth color to a palette that already has five competing near-duplicates (two neutral families, three accent purples); subtract before adding.
Don't reach for backdrop-filter: blur as the default answer to "how do I make this stand out." That is the single most repeated "AI slop" tell across these screens.

Don't build another one-off button, card, or input. The fact that DeleteVizButton is the only named component in the codebase is the root cause of "templated and interchangeable"; new screens should draw from a shared set, not add another local variant.
Don't let .prose keep its own isolated palette (stone grays, blue-400 links). Fold it into whatever single neutral and accent system replaces the current mix.
